intro: The clearest difference between Private AI and StackHawk is pricing — Private AI is usage-based, while StackHawk is subscription (no free tier). Beyond that, lock-in — Private AI has medium lock-in (Usage-based PII redaction API), whereas StackHawk has low lock-in (Based on OSS ZAP scanner). Private AI fits regulated industries and LLM apps needing PII redaction before storage, training, or inference; StackHawk fits marketplace, apps. On the strengths side, Private AI is cited for PII detection across modalities, while StackHawk is cited for UGC platforms (social, marketplace). The honest trade-off: Private AI is the wrong call for teams that just need toxicity, NSFW, or general UGC moderation; StackHawk is the wrong call for internal projects; Private AI users flag not a general content moderation tool, and StackHawk users flag B2B products without user content. Choosing between them should come down to the constraint that actually binds your workflow, not surface-level overlap.
quick_take: Private AI is for regulated industries and LLM apps needing; StackHawk is for marketplace, apps; decide based on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Private AI if your use case is regulated industries and LLM apps needing PII redaction before storage, training, or inference, and a usage-based pricing shape fits your budget better than subscription (no free tier).
verdict_b: Choose StackHawk if your use case is marketplace, apps, and a subscription (no free tier) pricing shape fits your budget better than usage-based.

faq.q: Is Private AI cheaper than StackHawk?
faq.a: Private AI is usage, while StackHawk is subscription, so the cheaper option depends on your usage shape. Listed pricing pages usually change, so check both directly before committing. For small teams, the free-tier limits often matter more than headline prices.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Private AI to StackHawk?
faq.a: Migrating from Private AI to StackHawk is feasible when their scopes overlap, but the work is in re-mapping configuration, data models, and integrations. Moving off Private AI is harder because of medium lock-in (Usage-based PII redaction API). Plan for a dual-run period so you can validate parity before decommissioning.

On our developer-experience score, StackHawk is rated 5/5 and Private AI 4/5, so StackHawk edges ahead. Scores like this smooth over real differences in docs, CLI feel, and SDK quality, so treat them as a starting point. Spend an afternoon in each before relying on the number.

faq.q: Is StackHawk a good alternative to Private AI?
faq.a: StackHawk is a reasonable alternative to Private AI when StackHawk fits marketplace, apps. If your use case matches Private AI's sweet spot — regulated industries and LLM apps needing PII redaction before storage, training, or inference — staying with Private AI is probably safer. Private AI itself is not meant for teams that just need toxicity, NSFW, or general UGC moderation, which is often where users start shopping for alternatives.
